resources.string: Select your games folder to build your library.
Your library, setup, and quick actions in one place.
Choose a BIOS file and your games folder to get started.
Try a different name or change the sorting.
Games currently shown in the library.
Shows whether the BIOS file is ready.
Shows whether a games folder is selected.
Game description and metadata on this screen are provided via the IGDB API.
No comments for this game yet.
This entry is shown from the local PS2 database. Add the game file to your library if you want to launch it.
We could not match this game to the local PS2 catalog yet.
PS2 catalog details are temporarily unavailable.
Write the current state to the quick slot.
Restore the latest state from the quick slot.
This will overwrite slot %1$d with the current game state.
This will restore slot %1$d and replace your current unsaved progress.
Manage the current session without leaving the game.
The previous emulation session was closed in the background. Launch the game again from your library.
Achievements are only available while a game is loaded. BIOS boot does not expose RetroAchievements data.
RetroAchievements are not supported in the current native build.
No RetroAchievements data is available for this game right now.
%1$d more achievements in the full game list.
Choose the interface language for EmuCoreX.
Selecting a driver file here will switch the GPU driver to Custom automatically.
Sign in to track achievements, hardcore mode, and rich presence while you play supported games.
Turn on RetroAchievements integration for supported games.
Use stricter RetroAchievements rules for hardcore and leaderboard progress.
You are not signed in to RetroAchievements yet.
Launch a supported game to load achievements and rich presence.
Enable RetroAchievements to start tracking supported games.
Your saved RetroAchievements session expired. Please sign in again.
A dedicated RetroAchievements hub for your account and games.
Sign in to RetroAchievements to load your unlocked achievements across the library.
No unlocked achievements found in the scanned library yet.
Your synced unlocks grouped by game.
Use your RetroAchievements account here instead of going through Settings.
Unable to load achievements for this game right now.
The full list is available without sign-in, but unlocked marks sync only after account login.
There are no achievements for this game yet.
This game was recognized, but its achievement list is not available right now.
Select the PS2 BIOS image file needed to start games.
Pick your PlayStation 2 BIOS image file. This file will be used for normal game boot and BIOS boot.
Select the folder that contains your games.
Paste one or two direct base URLs. If you use two, put the 2D URL first and the 3D URL second, separated by a space. Leave it empty to use the built-in source again.
Tap to paste both example URLs. Long press to copy them.
Enter a valid HTTP or HTTPS URL.
EmuCoreX is a PlayStation 2 library and launcher built around a PCSX2-based emulation core, with quick setup, clean navigation, and a handheld-friendly experience.
Open the official EmuCoreX website for updates, information, and useful links.

EmuCoreX combines its own Android interface and library features with an emulation core derived from the open-source PCSX2 project. The bridge was also refined using the ARMSX2 team\'s experience and updates, and their help was invaluable during development.
Support ongoing development and future updates for EmuCoreX.
Open the author page on Google Play to see other apps from Sashkin Apps.
Enable vibration in supported games and on supported devices.
Display the frame rate while playing.
Display the recently played games section on the home screen.
Display the search bar on the home screen.
Use English names from the PCSX2 game database when they are available.

Automatically switches to a connected controller when one is detected. Keep it on if you move between touch controls and a pad often; turn it off only if you want to keep the current input layout fixed.
Prevents Android from dimming or locking the screen during play. Useful for long sessions and games with cutscenes, but it will use a little more battery if you leave the app open.

Lets supported games trigger controller rumble. It adds useful feedback in racing, combat, and impact-heavy games, but it can drain battery faster and some wireless pads may vibrate weakly or with slight delay.
Shows a quick-access row for the games you played last. Good if you bounce between a few titles often; turn it off if you want a cleaner home screen.
Shows the library search bar directly on the home screen. Leave it on for big libraries; turn it off if you prefer a cleaner layout and rarely search by name.

Uses English titles from PCSX2\'s GameIndex database for games that ship with Japanese or other regional names. This mainly helps imported releases read more clearly in the library, while games without an English database title keep their normal name.

Points EmuCoreX to your PS2 BIOS image file. A clean dump from your own console is the best choice. The app expects the main BIOS image, usually a .bin or .rom file, and keeps any generated NVRAM/MEC files beside the imported copy.

Selects the library folder that EmuCoreX scans for games. The scan is recursive, so subfolders are fine. Supported images include .iso, .bin, .chd, .cso, and .gz. ISO is the safest baseline, while CHD is usually the best long-term format because it saves space without giving up normal usage.

Chooses the default cover type used when downloading artwork. Flat covers match the classic front-box look, while 3D Box uses angled box renders when the source provides them. 3D covers can look more dramatic, but availability may be slightly worse than flat art.

Overrides the built-in cover source used for automatic downloads. The URL should point directly to a folder that contains JPG covers named by game serial, for example SLUS-20312.jpg. Change this only if you host your own mirror or want to use a different compatible cover library.

Selects the graphics backend used for hardware rendering. Vulkan is usually the fastest on modern devices, OpenGL can be safer for compatibility on some GPUs, and Software is mainly a fallback for testing or games that break badly in hardware mode.

Raises internal render resolution above native PS2 output. 3D scenes look much cleaner, but GPU load climbs fast and some games show extra seams, blur, or broken effects at higher values. If a game looks wrong, switch back to Native first.

Controls how the final image is framed on your display. Auto follows the game, 4:3 keeps the original look for most PS2 titles, 16:9 suits widescreen-patched games, and Stretch should only be used if you do not mind geometric distortion.

Sharpens textures viewed at steep angles, especially roads, floors, fields, and long walls. It usually has a smaller cost than raising resolution, so it is a good quality upgrade if the game already runs well.

Applies a lightweight post-process anti-aliasing pass after rendering. It can smooth jagged edges in games that shimmer, but it also softens UI text and fine detail, so use it only if the cleaner image is worth the blur.

Applies Contrast Adaptive Sharpening after rendering. It is useful if upscaling, FXAA, or bilinear filtering makes the image too soft, but strong sharpening can create halos or noisy edges in some games.
Sets how aggressive the CAS pass looks. Lower values give a subtle recovery of detail, while higher values make edges pop more but can also introduce ringing and harsh outlines.

Loads widescreen patches when the current game has one available. This is usually better than stretching the image because it changes the game’s camera and HUD logic instead of just distorting the final picture.
Applies no-interlacing patches where available. This is worth trying in games with combing, shimmer, or flicker from original interlaced output, but availability depends on the title.
Changes the size of on-screen buttons and sticks. Increase it if touch accuracy matters more than screen space; lower it if the overlay covers too much of the image.
Controls how solid or transparent the touch controls look. Lower opacity keeps the game view cleaner, while higher opacity makes buttons easier to see in dark or busy scenes.
Hides the touch overlay whenever a physical controller is active. This makes the screen cleaner, but turn it off if you still want touch buttons available while a controller is connected.
Uses a tighter overlay layout that takes less space around the edges of the screen. Good for smaller phones, but some players may prefer the larger spacing of the standard layout.
Changes the size of the analog stick touch areas. Larger values make them easier to grab during action-heavy games, while smaller values leave more room for the picture and nearby buttons.

Applies a tested group of graphics and speed settings in one step. Start here before tuning manually: lower presets favor stability and battery, while faster presets trade image quality or compatibility for speed.

Keeps game speed tied to the selected target frame rate instead of running as fast as possible. Leave it enabled for normal play; disable it only for testing, uncapped behavior, or very specific use cases.

Skips presenting identical frames when the game keeps drawing the same image repeatedly. It can lower GPU work and input latency in some 25/30 FPS titles, but it may worsen frame pacing or timing in games that expect every present, so leave it off unless a game clearly benefits.
Sets the speed target used by the limiter. Most games should stay at their native cap, so change this only if you know the game or patch supports a different target safely.

Adjusts how much EE CPU time the emulator gives the game each frame. Lower values can rescue borderline performance on weak devices, but they also make game timing less accurate and can cause slowdown, audio issues, or broken logic.

Lets the emulator skip part of the EE workload to gain speed. It is stronger and riskier than Cycle Rate: useful when a game is close to full speed, but aggressive values can cause stutter, input delay, missing effects, or unstable behavior.
Runs VU1 work on another CPU thread. This often helps on modern multi-core devices, especially in heavier 3D games, but a few titles may show instability or odd timing with it enabled.

Uses looser virtual disc timing to shorten load-related overhead. It can improve performance and reduce long loads in some games, but titles that depend on stricter timing may hang, loop, or fail to load certain scenes correctly.

Controls how the hardware renderer handles GPU readbacks. Faster modes reduce stalls and can help performance in lightweight scenes, but they are more likely to break games that read the framebuffer for shadows, post-processing, or HUD effects. If a game has missing effects, try a safer mode.

Controls how closely hardware rendering matches PS2 alpha blending. Raise it if you see broken fog, halos, light shafts, ghosting, or missing transparency. Lower levels are faster, so leave it low unless the game shows real blending bugs.

Preloads textures before the game asks for them. It can reduce streaming pop-in or stutter in texture-heavy areas, but higher levels use more memory and may do nothing for simpler games. Best left modest unless a game clearly benefits.

Enables mipmaps in the hardware renderer when the game uses them correctly. This can reduce shimmer and improve texture stability in the distance, but a few games have mipmap bugs and may look better with it off.

Drops rendered frames when the device cannot keep up. It can make a slow game feel more playable, but animation becomes rougher and frame pacing gets worse, so it should be a last resort rather than a default tweak.

Loads enabled cheat patches for the current game. Useful for widescreen, fan fixes, translations, or gameplay cheats, but the wrong patch version can break stability or apply to the wrong release of a game.
Shows a live performance overlay while the game is running. Use it when tuning settings or checking whether a slowdown comes from emulation speed, frame pacing, or the game itself.
Chooses between a simple FPS readout and a more detailed stats panel. Use the compact mode if you just want a quick speed check, or the extended mode when you are actively tuning performance.
Places the FPS or stats overlay in one of the four corners. Move it away from touch controls, HUD elements, subtitles, or minimaps so it stays readable without covering important game info.

Selects which GPU driver package to use when custom driver switching is available. The system driver is the safest default; change this only if you are testing a known driver that improves performance or fixes rendering on your specific GPU.

Controls the main texture filtering mode used by the renderer. Nearest keeps the sharpest raw look, PS2 stays closest to original console behavior, and Forced modes smooth more aggressively. If a game looks too blocky or too blurry, this is the first filtering option to adjust.

Controls how textures are filtered before they reach the screen. PS2/Automatic modes stay closer to original behavior, Forced modes smooth everything, and Nearest keeps the sharpest but roughest image. If a game looks too blurry or too noisy, this is one of the first options to try.

Smooths transitions between mip levels on textures that use them. It can reduce shimmer on roads, walls, and distant surfaces, but stronger modes may blur the image more than the original game intended.

Sets the sprite size threshold where draws start moving to the CPU path. This is mainly useful for games with broken 2D effects, shadows, or post-processing at higher resolutions. Raising it can fix more issues, but it costs CPU performance quickly.
Controls how aggressively sprite rendering is offloaded to the CPU. Higher levels can repair more sprite-based effects, but they are heavier and should usually be used only as a per-game fix.

Processes CLUT and palette work through a software path instead of the usual GPU route. Useful for wrong colors, broken gradients, subtitles, or palette-heavy 2D games, but slower than normal hardware rendering.

Controls whether the renderer builds CLUT textures directly from GPU targets. Safer modes help games that render their palettes into textures first; faster modes reduce overhead but can miss those effects.

Flushes hardware draws more often so later passes can read a finished result. It often helps missing bloom, shadows, framebuffer effects, or bad layering, but it can hurt performance a lot if left on globally.

Skips draw calls from the start of a chosen range. Use it only for specific games with black boxes, ghost layers, broken blur, or bad post-processing. Start low and increase carefully, because it can also remove effects you actually want.
Sets the end of the skipped draw-call range. It works together with Skip Draw Start and is meant for surgical, game-specific fixes rather than general tuning.

Changes how the hardware renderer detects textures stored inside render targets. This can fix missing HUDs, videos, or framebuffer-based effects in some games, but stronger modes are heavier and should only be used when needed.

Adjusts sampling to compensate for half-pixel rounding differences between the PS2 and modern graphics APIs. It is often the first fix to try for shifted bloom, double images, outlines, or post-processing that looks offset when upscaling.

Keeps certain sprite and 2D draws closer to native PS2 scaling even when the game is rendered above native resolution. This can help HUD alignment, text, and post-process layers that only break when the image is upscaled.
Rounds sprite coordinates to reduce thin seams, gaps, or flickering lines on 2D elements and HUDs. It is especially useful in games that show grid lines only when rendered above native resolution.
Applies bilinear filtering to upscale passes used by some effects. It can smooth harsh scaling artifacts in post-process layers, but it may also soften the image more than you want.
Offsets texture sampling horizontally. This is a precision fix for games where text, HUDs, or post-process layers appear shifted left or right after upscaling.
Offsets texture sampling vertically. Use it only when a game shows layers, HUD elements, or effects shifted up or down after changing resolution.

Moves 4-bit and 8-bit framebuffer conversion from the GPU to the CPU. This can fix some HUDs, shadows, FMV overlays, and other framebuffer tricks, but it is expensive and is best used only for games that clearly need it.

Stops depth data from being converted in the texture cache. It can fix a few games with incorrect lighting, dark scenes, or performance drops, but it may also break effects that rely on correct depth information.

Turns off conservative safety checks in the hardware renderer. It can help with oversized effects, thick outlines, or upscaling glitches in a few games, but it also increases the chance of new rendering bugs. Leave it off unless you are fixing a specific issue.

Disables automatic per-game render fixes from the core. Usually you should leave this off, because those fixes exist for a reason. Turn it on only if an automatic fix is clearly causing a new problem and you want to test without it.

Uploads GS memory at the start of the frame instead of waiting for the effect to request it later. This can fix difficult framebuffer effects such as missing lava, heat haze, or special blending, but it increases bandwidth and may reduce performance.

Invalidates the whole overlapping texture region instead of only the intersected part. This can help games with stale textures, broken post-processing, or framebuffer feedback issues, but it is heavier on bandwidth and cache use.

Forces render targets back to local memory when they are closed. Useful for games that lose effects after scene changes, menus, or transitions, but it is slower and usually only worth enabling per game.
Guesses the real size of textures when the game does not define the region properly. It can fix stretched, oversized, or garbage textures, but if the guess is wrong it can create new artifacts.

Moves palette conversion work from the CPU to the GPU. It is usually faster, especially in palette-heavy 2D games, but if a title shows wrong colors or broken CLUT effects, try the safer path instead.

Adjusts sprite placement to hide thin vertical seams that appear when 2D elements are upscaled. This is one of the classic fixes for UI lines, menu borders, and sprite edges in games that are otherwise fine at native resolution.

Combines neighboring post-process sprites into a larger draw so bloom, blur, and similar fullscreen effects line up better when upscaled. It can fix split or duplicated effects, but sometimes it over-corrects and changes the look.
Snaps sprite positions to even coordinates to reduce tiny gaps or shimmering between pixels at higher resolutions. Helpful for 2D-heavy games, but it can slightly shift some elements.

Draws palette-based effects closer to native precision. This can repair color-indexed effects, palette shadows, or other CLUT-heavy tricks that break when upscaled, but it may also make high-resolution output look less clean.
Use a tighter layout for on-screen controls.
Prevent the screen from dimming while playing.
Keeps your manual tuning. The preset switches back to Custom when you change a tuned option.
Lower heat and power draw. Best for long sessions on weaker MediaTek devices.
Recommended starting point for most MediaTek devices. Good image quality without heavy speed hacks.
Adds moderate speed hacks and keeps image quality solid. Good when Balanced is close but not stable enough.
Pushes speed hacks harder for difficult games. Faster, but more likely to cause glitches or unstable timing.
Allows the core to load active cheats from pnach files.
Add a .pnach file and manage it directly in the app.
Tip: name cheat files by game serial or serial plus CRC for best in-game matching.
This adjusts renderer and safe speedhacks for the current device family without hardcoding per-game hacks.
Recommended for MediaTek: switches to OpenGL, keeps native resolution, lowers filtering load, and uses safer speedhacks to reduce crashes and improve frame pacing.
Recommended for Snapdragon: keeps the fast default path and reapplies the balanced preset for stable performance.
Applies a balanced compatibility preset for devices that do not have a chipset-specific profile yet.
Uses a second thread for VU1 - speeds up most games
Faster disc access - may cause issues in some games
Changes synchronization behavior for hardware GS downloads. Accurate is the safest option.
Keeps the game closer to the selected target speed instead of running fully uncapped.
Avoids presenting identical frames multiple times in a row.
Enables emulation of the GS texture mipmapping path.
Convert 4-bit and 8-bit framebuffers on the CPU instead of the GPU.
Turns off depth buffer support in the texture cache.
Turns off several conservative safety checks for speed.
Disables automatic per-game render fixes from the core.
Uploads GS data at the start of a new frame for difficult effects.
Invalidates the full overlapping texture region instead of only the intersected area.
Flushes render targets back to local memory on shutdown.
Tries to reduce oversized textures in games that do not specify their region well.
Moves palette conversion work to the GPU instead of the CPU.
Helps with vertical seam issues when upscaling some games.
Merges multiple post-process sprites into a larger one.
Lowers precision to avoid gaps between pixels on upscaled output.
Can fix effects that depend on pixel-perfect palette precision.
No cheats were found for this game.
Enable widescreen patches for supported games
Enable no-interlacing patches for supported games
Are you sure you want to reset all settings? This will restore the default app configuration.
Automatically map buttons when a gamepad is connected
Hide touch controls when a physical gamepad is connected
Connect a physical gamepad to test and remap buttons.
%1$s controller is not connected. You can still configure its mapping.
Restore the default physical button layout.
Press a button on the connected controller to bind it to %1$s.
Press a button for %1$s to bind it to %2$s.
Your PlayStation 2 library and launcher designed for quick setup, clean navigation, and a streamlined handheld-friendly experience.
Experience your favorite PS2 games with advanced graphics options, customizable controls, and performance optimizations.
Build your personal game library with beautiful covers, detailed information, and quick access to your favorites.
Add a BIOS file and choose your games folder to get started.
Safe is recommended for most devices and games. Fast can improve speed on weaker phones, but it may reduce compatibility and stability.
Best balance for most mid-range and high-end devices. Keeps stronger compatibility with games.
For weaker devices that need extra speed. Some games may show more glitches or become less stable.
Add a BIOS file and choose your games folder.
Select the PS2 BIOS image file needed to start games.
Select the folder that contains your games.
%1$d of %2$d setup steps are complete. Once setup is finished, your games will appear in the library.
Not required when your games folder was selected through Android folder access. Raw folders still need this so EmuCoreX can read serials and load game covers correctly.
Grant storage access so EmuCoreX can correctly read game serials, match GitHub covers, and launch games from raw library folders without permission errors.
The PS2 catalog is temporarily unavailable right now.
Try a different game name or a shorter search phrase.
EmuCoreX supports the main image formats used for PlayStation 2 games.
These formats can appear in your library.
For the best experience, use ISO or CHD. BIN also works for some disc dumps.
Convert an ISO image to CHD to save storage space.
ISO to CHD conversion is not available in this build.
Ready to pick an ISO image.
CHD is ready. Choose where to save it.
Please choose an `.iso` file for conversion.
We could not convert this image right now. Try another ISO or try again in a moment.
The CHD file was saved successfully.
The converted CHD could not be saved to the selected location.
Move buttons and change analog stick size.
Drag buttons to move • Slider to resize sticks
Export emulator settings, per-game profiles, cheat selections, and memory cards.
Restore a previously exported EmuCoreX settings backup.
Open the full list of achievements for this game.
Open the full list of achievements for this game. Sign in later if you want earned marks to sync.
Account unlocks synced for this game
Create a save in-game first, then it will appear here.
This game does not have any saves yet.
Manage loading, deletion, backup, and restore from one place.
Game file not found in the library
Manage PS2 memory cards, slots, backup, restore, and storage.
Assign cards to the two standard PS2 slots, create new cards, export single cards, make full backups, restore existing cards, and manage duplicates or renames safely.
Assign cards to the standard two slots and manage backups from one place.
Failed to create the memory card.
Failed to back up memory cards.
Failed to export the memory card.
Failed to duplicate the memory card.
Failed to rename the memory card.
%1$s will be deleted permanently. Assigned slots will be cleared automatically.
Failed to delete the memory card.
Failed to update the memory card slots.
Create a new card or restore one from a backup to get started.
Review slots, create new cards, and manage backups.

Applies the core post-processing image controls after the frame is rendered. Use it to tune washed-out scenes, over-bright games, or displays that need a little more punch without touching the rest of the renderer.
Raises or lowers the overall light level of the final image. Use small changes here first if the picture looks too dark or too flat.
Controls the separation between bright and dark areas. Higher values make highlights and shadows stronger, while lower values soften the image.
Adjusts how intense the colors look. Increase it if a game appears dull, or lower it if the output becomes too vivid or noisy.
Shifts mid-tones without pushing the whole image as hard as brightness. It is useful when menus or gameplay look slightly grey or slightly crushed.
Tune the final image without changing the core renderer path. These controls are great for fixing dull, harsh, or overly dark output on a per-game basis.
Long-press the label of any setting to restore its default value. This works in Settings, in the Game Manager, and in the in-game menu, so you can quickly undo changes while testing.
Manage per-game profiles, edit core settings, and move them between devices.
Export, import, or reset every per-game profile without touching your global defaults.
Every saved per-game settings profile will be removed. Global app settings will stay untouched.
Failed to export game settings profiles.
Failed to import game settings profiles.
Start tuning a game in the overlay and a dedicated profile will appear here automatically.
%1$s will go back to your global defaults.
These values override your global defaults for this game only.
This game uses its own saved settings profile.
This game is currently using your global default settings.
Changes made in the in-game menu are stored for this game instead of changing every title.
No save states found for this game.
